segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Listen to a talk in an archaeology class.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Imagine an ancient garden or field, and imagine organic material in its soil, things like plant roots or wooden irrigation pipes.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: When this organic material decays over the centuries, it often leaves behind voids in the soil.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Researchers can fill these voids by injecting plaster into the empty spaces.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: When the plaster hardens, it creates casts, essentially three-dimensional negatives that replicate what existed centuries ago.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: For example, at the ancient Maya village of Joya de Ceren in El Salvador, Central America, casts taken from root cavities have revealed a diverse mix of crops, including manioc, maize, and beans.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: These species were identified in part by the shape and size of the root voids by comparing them with modern plant root systems.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: This layout of plants in the garden wasn't random at all.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: It showed the garden to be an early example of the milpa system, a traditional intercropping method still practiced in Central America and Mexico today, where the tall maize plants provide stalks for bean plants to climb, the beans enrich the soil with nitrogen, and squash plants growing along the ground provide shade that suppresses weeds and retains moisture.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Soil casts have also been used at the sites of ancient Roman villa gardens, revealing irrigation channels and beds planted with species such as grapevines and roses.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: The findings show a deliberate integration of ornamental plants with edible plants, suggesting a Roman design ethos that sought to balance aesthetics with utility.
